summary: Gruber examines Apple's track record with acquired apps, arguing that Apple generally treats acquisitions well — either keeping apps alive (Logic, Shazam), folding technology into the OS (Siri, Dark Sky, Workflow/Shortcuts), or both. He uses this history to predict the future of the recently announced Pixelmator acquisition, concluding that Pixelmator is too good to scrap and will likely become an Apple-branded Photoshop competitor alongside Final Cut Pro and Logic Pro. He contrasts Apple's approach with industry norms where acquisitions often lead to neglect or destruction of beloved apps, and notes Apple has never done a purely anti-competitive acquisition to kill a product.
key_points: Apple's acquisition outcomes fall into clear categories: keep the app going (Logic, Shazam), fold technology into the OS (Siri, Dark Sky), or a hybrid approach (Workflow/Shortcuts) — and Apple rarely does pure acquihires or anti-competitive shutdowns
Pixelmator is too good to scrap and would slot naturally alongside Final Cut Pro and Logic Pro as an Apple pro tool, likely becoming an Apple-branded Photoshop competitor for Mac and iPad
The PA Semi acquisition for $278 million was far more important than the $3 billion Beats deal, as it paved the path for Apple Silicon
Apple's acquisition of NeXT for $400 million was arguably the most impactful acquisition in the entire history of computing
Gruber is less certain about Photomator's future, noting Apple walked away from the Lightroom-competing market when it discontinued Aperture a decade ago
money_quotes: Apple respects the art of making great apps.
It's commonplace in the industry for a large company to acquire a small company that makes a very cool app, and then somehow ruin that app.
An examination of Apple's acquisition history doesn't give me any reason for alarm. Apple really does tend to do right by cool app acquisitions.
Facebook Paper was so good, so forward-thinking, so innovative, that it almost got me to create a Facebook account for the first time.
Anyone who liked Workflow surely loves Shortcuts — it's far more powerful and capable as an OS-level technology from Apple than it ever could have hoped to have been as a third-party app.

The largest acquisition by importance in Apple's history, of course, was their $400 million deal to reunify the company with NeXT at the end of 1996. That's beyond dispute. That was arguably the most impactful acquisition in the entire history of computing.
I still can't fully forgive Adobe for doing this with FreeHand when they acquired Macromedia in 2005.
key_insight: Apple's acquisition history shows a consistent pattern of respecting and preserving the value of acquired apps, making the Pixelmator acquisition more likely to produce a new Apple-branded pro tool than a shuttered product.
